comments: End of show missing. Tommy announces Thin Lizzy at the top of the show, there is a hint of crowd noise as the tape finishes so the missing track could be something from Live And Dangerous.

sessions.details: First Broadcast On Bob Harris' Show on 4th September 1972. (Note: Ken Garner's In Session book describes the artist as Peter Frampton's Band)

track_listing.track: Do You Feel Like I Do

track_listing.details: LP-Welcome To The Wrecking Ball; RCA Victor
LP-Bringing It All Back Home; Columbia

track_listing.track: 'Whole Lotta Rosie (LP-If You Want Blood You've Got It)' (Atlantic)

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. This is TV on the Radio. Here Thomas the Vance. And welcome once again to the program we call The Friday Rock Show.
Right on our program tonight from Witchfind we have their first ever Radio 1 session.
And also in our archive, Swats, you'll hear the only session ever recorded by Peter Frampton's Camel.
That was put together at the BBC in 1972.
And we've got some new stuff by Iron Maiden, by Grace Slick, by Todd Rundgren, classics from Tommy Bolan, Bob Dylan, Dave Gilmour, ACDC and Thin Lizzy.
It's Friday the 13th, just in case you had forgotten, and Wilson's already set the studio on fire.
But we'll do our best. We also have our Friday Night Connection, but we start with this.
And that's a track from an album that's coming out this Monday, to the best of my knowledge.
The album is called Killers. The band are Iron Maiden, the track you heard was Prodigal Son.
Before that, something by Black Sabbath for everybody in Black Sabbath,
from a load of people who wrote in all about that sort of strange gig
that happened out of the Sophia Gardens in Cardiff
when there were too many tickets for the number of people
who wanted to get into the hall.
So Sabbath actually did two performances that night.
The track I played, of course, was Neon Nights.
Just to mention a couple of people who wrote in full of praise for the band.
Mark Davis of Mumbles in Swansea,
David Morgan, who lives in Divert in South Wales,
and Angela Doyle, who lives in Swansea in West Glamorgan.
Sabbath track was also for somebody on a Valentine basis.
Dear TV, please would you play any good heavy metal track preferably?
Black Sabbath for Sean, the hippie, and Ada and Big Rachel myself.
Myself being, in this instance, Cathy Pottet.
I think it's Lopset in Wakefield.
40 minutes after the hour of ten, I've got a nutty letter here
from a gentleman by the name of Vitek Shaw.
That's a Polish name, he tells me.
loads of PS's
and also a list of why the Friday Rock Show is tops
which he says is a terrible word
I think you're wonderful Mr Vance is reason one
reason two Tony Wilson is great
reason three I like the music you play
reason four my wife loves Ronnie James Dio
Rock's answer to Lucy Ewing
reason five filthy animal Taylor likes
hanging around outside gents toilets

reason six am I making sense
reason seven did he hear about the Irish Sea Scout
his tent sank
reason eight the reporters in Soundscan
off and his joy john mccoy's hair his own that's reason nine and reason 10 was that he wanted to
win the connection but he didn't but he likes hearing his name so it actual how are you and
here now our witch find which find where are you little witch find which find is actually going to
start about now well i don't think it is so we'll forget about wish wine for a minute and we'll play

from the album private eyes and the track was nine minutes and five seconds of absolute sheer
delight that has the title post toasty friday the 13th isn't it but we still have which fine
which fine and the first of four that you're going to hear from our session by them tonight
That was called Getting Heavy.
And prior to that, you heard something by Tommy and Bolin,
but I already did that announcement, so let's just do the dedications.
The Witchvine track was for the vampire family,
who sent me some human blood, it says here.
Please open after dark.
In actual fact, it's only tomato ketchup.
Now, the Bolin track was for Pete Beaumont,
who normally lives in Bath, but at the moment he's, uh,
he's down there in the middle of a very large area
in Yelverton in Devon having a bit of a holiday.
Hope everything goes well.
Keep your nose clean, Pete, and you get out faster.
Nice letter from Nick Jackson, who lives in New Malden in Surrey, says,
Can I mention all the Bolin fans in and around that area,
even though there seems to be a distinct lack of them,
especially Colin the Teaser in Bolton in Lancs,
and for Simon Robinson, who runs the Deep Purple Appreciation Society.
Also mention all the heavy metal freaks, all five of them,
at the CYFA in New Malden.
Hope you can play it. We did, and it was our pleasure, Nick.
It's exactly 10.30 on the Friday Rock Show from BBC Radio 1,
Recorded on the 23rd of the 8th, 1972.
A couple of numbers by Frampton's Camel.
Well, in my humble opinion, that's the best version of that I've ever heard.
It was actually recorded in the BBC Studios way back in 1972.
50% of our archive session by Frampton's Camel there.
That was Do You Feel Like I Do?
Before that, you heard them play It's a Plain Shame.
Dedicated specially to two, according to this letter from Dave Evans, it is.
And it is Dave Edmonds, Freudian slip.
Two wonderful young ladies, Karen and Sarah, who live in Denham in Buckinghamshire.
Missy Dave thinks I'm unobvious, quite a long letter.
Also want to say hello to Anne Halligan and Margaret Elliott.
Sarah Dequeenen, I think it is, Julie Davies, Eileen Atkinson,
and all the hard-working third years at the School of Pharmacy in the University of London.
Karen Fowler has a 22-year-old birthday coming up on February the 25th.
Happy birthday to her, wherever she says, or wherever she is, rather.
Did a gig at the London School of Pharmacy the other night.
Best gig I think I've ever been to in all my life.
Thank you very much for the hospitality.
It was great.
Also, hello to Steve and Georgina and everybody else who attends the Square Disco.
Long live High Wycombe and the Jones Boys.
Janet and Pam Neill and everybody on the rank committee at the London School of Pharmacy deserve a mention too.
19 to 11, there's a new album out by Grace Slick.
Here's the track.
...of listening to that album.
I think it's about number four on our charts at the moment.
Dire Straits from the album Making Movies.
The track was called Skate Away, played especially for Spider and Swift Nick, it says here.
Thunderclap and Captain Woody and Smallkey and Tom and Wookie and Melanie
from a gentleman by the name of Chris Bommer, who lives in Lancaster.
And also to all the members of the 11th Norwich Sea Scouts Venture Unit,
who were at Baden-Powell House in London on February the 7th and the 8th.
And they were Barry Vick and Chris Kemp, Sean Bothorpe, Mac Cubitt, Andrew Shorten,
David Taylor, Philip Brooksby and Mark Middleton.
and before that you heard Grace Slick, a track from her new album.
The album is called Welcome to the Wrecking Ball
and the track was entitled The Sea of Love.
Bit of name-dropping here.
I was talking to a couple of the guys out of Dire Straits this morning.
I was actually talking to John and to Hal
and as Hal left the studio within which we were in Scots,
he said, hey, do me a favour, play me your record, will you?
And this is the one he wanted to hear.
So, Hal, this is for you.
The man mixing up the medicine, I'm on the pavement
Thinking about the government
The man in a trench coat, batch out, laid off
Says he's got a bad cough, wants to get it paid off
Look out, kid, it's something you did
God knows when, but you're doing it again
You better duck down the alleyway, looking for a new friend
The man in the coon skin cap and a pig pen
Wants eleven dollar bills, you only got ten
Maggie calls Fleetwood, space full of black soot
Talking at the heat, put plants in the bed
But the post taps anyway, Maggie says the men
They say they must bust an early man, orders from the D.A.
Look out, kid, don't matter what you did
But walk on your tiptoes, don't tie no bows
Better stay away from those that carry around a fire hose
Keep a clean nose, wash a clean coat
You don't need a weatherman to know it's where the wind blows
Get sick, get well, hang around the inkwell
Hang a bell, hard to tell if anything is gonna sell
Try hard, get far, get back right frail
Get jailed, jump bail, join the army if you fail
Look out, kid, you're gonna get hit
By losers, cheaters, six-time users, hangin' round the beaters
Girl by the world, fool's lookin' for a new fool
Don't follow leaders, watch your parking meters
Keep on, keep on, short pants romance
Learn to dance, get dressed, get flipped
Try to be a success, please her, please him
Buy gifts, don't steal, don't list
20 years of schoolin' and it puts you on the day shift
Look out, kid, they keep it all hid
Better jump down the manhole, light yourself a candle
Don't wear sandals, try to avoid the scandal
Don't want to be a bum, you better chew gum
The pump don't work, cause the vandals took the handle
